w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: App Deep Feature Analysis for Gym Log App Design

positions itself as a simple but powerful workout tracker with three pillars: workout logging, progress tracking, and social/community features. It offers an extensive routine library, detailed performance analytics, and social tools that make tracking strength training engaging for over 4–12 million users across platforms.[1][2][3][4][5]
feature set and main screens from official documentation, app store listings, and third

party reviews, then translates them into a concrete feature checklist you can apply to your own gym log app. While internal-only or minor UI screens are not fully documented publicly, the available information is sufficient to infer a robust, production
grade feature set for a competitive lifting tracker.[3][6][5]
markets itself as a free, ad
free gym tracker and planner focused on people who lift weights and want a clean way to log workouts,
progression, and leverage social motivation. The core positioning is
simplest, most intuitive workout tracker in the world
- **Workout logging** – fast routine planning, easy set logging, exercise library, custom exercises.
level analytics, body metrics, gym calendar, streaks.
- **Socializing** – content feed, athlete profiles, following, comments, likes, comparisons, and shareables.
For your own app, matching these pillars (logging, analytics, social) is key if you want to be in the same class as
Based on feature documentation and walkthroughs,
main navigation is built around tabbed sections (names are from the docs, not necessarily exact labels in the UI):[7][8][9][10]
- **Home / Social Feed** – scrollable feed of workouts from people you follow, with likes, comments, media, and quick stats.
- **Discover / Explore** – discovery feed and suggested athletes, plus options to view athlete profiles and routines.
- **Workout Tab** – central training hub to start a workout, view and manage routines, and access the routine library/programs.
- **Profile Tab** – personal dashboard with stats, calendar, history, body metrics, progress photos, and advanced analytics.
* – dedicated app + backend for trainers managing
- **Settings & Extras** – privacy (public/private profile), integrations (HealthKit, Strava, Google Fit), app preferences, widgets, theme,
Your gym log app can reuse this mental model: a training tab, a profile/stats tab, and (optionally) social/home and discover tabs.
## Workout Logging: Routines, Exercises, and Set Entry
includes an “advanced routine planner” and separate “log workout” experience that share the same core structure:[2][3]
- Shows saved routines (e.g., PPL, full body, 3
day split) as cards or rows.[3]
- Supports multiple training types: strength, bodybuilding, 5x5, powerlifting, CrossFit, calisthenics, HIIT,
- Free tier supports a small number of custom routines; Pro unlocks unlimited routines.[6][3]
- Add, remove, and reorder exercises in a routine.[12][2]
exercise sets, rep ranges, and rest timers.[2][3]
- Mark sets as warmup, normal, drop set, failure, or superset.[6][3]
- Optionally pair exercises into supersets and adjust set types mid
- Displays current routine with exercises, sets, target reps/weight, and previous performance for each set.[3][6]
style inputs per set (weight, reps, RPE if enabled) and set type (warmup, normal, drop, failure, superset).[8][7][3]
fill from previous session to reduce typing friction (heavily praised by users).[6]
- Automatic rest timer per exercise, configurable (start automatically after set, vibrate on end, etc.).[3][6]
- Option to quickly add/remove sets or swap exercises without breaking the workout structure.[2][12]
workout exercise info: animated video and instruction text for exercises from the library.[6][3]
), a mirror of this screen with minimal controls for sets, reps, and rest timers.[3][6]
ships with “hundreds of exercises” and high
quality videos plus the ability to create custom exercises:[6][3]
- Filter by muscle group (e.g., chest, back, quads) and equipment type (barbell, dumbbell, machine, bodyweight, etc.).[3]
- Each exercise card shows name, target muscles, and equipment.
- Exercise detail view includes a video, step
- Define exercise name, primary and secondary muscle groups, equipment, and icon/image.[6][3]
- Custom exercises integrate fully into routines, stats, and graphs.[13]
emphasizes that the same logging UI supports many training styles (heavy lifting, powerlifting, bodybuilding, calisthenics, HIIT, etc.) and splits (PPL, upper/lower, 5x5, 3
day split, etc.). This mostly comes from flexible routines and free
For your app, the key is to have a generic model (exercise, set, rep, load, metadata) that can represent multiple training types without special cases.
has a sizable routine/program library that users can browse and import:[2]
- Accessed via the Workout tab under “Routines” → “Explore”.[2]
- Shows top programs and a button to view all (e.g., “Show all 26 programs”).[2]
- Filters: experience level, goal (muscle gain, strength, weight loss), and equipment availability.[2]
- Displays overview: level, goal, equipment, duration, and training frequency.[2]
- Lists included workouts (e.g., PPL days) with titles and descriptions.
- Provides a “Save Program” button to import the entire program into the user’s routine library.[2]
- Lists exercises with sets, rep ranges, rest times, and optional notes/tips per exercise.[2]
- Includes alternatives for certain movements and built
- Allows saving individual routines to the user’s own library (without importing the whole program) or sharing them outside the
- Categories such as At Home, Travel, Dumbbells Only, Band, Cardio & HIIT, Gym, Bodyweight, Suspension Band.[2]
- Each category lists 4–8 routines tailored to that context.[2]
This library is a major “value add” and a strong differentiator versus barebones loggers.
The Profile tab acts as the personal analytics and history hub:[15][10][7]
- Profile header with avatar, bio, and high
level stats (number of workouts, followers, following).[8][15]
- Buttons to access Statistics, Exercises, body metrics, and possibly progress photos.[7][11]
- List of saved workouts (newest first) with likes, comments, and quick stats.[10]
exposes a calendar and streak system that is prominent in the profile/analytics area:[10]
- Shows a calendar heatmap or marked days where workouts were logged.[10]
- Displays an “active streak” counter (consecutive weeks with at least one workout).[10]
- Allows zooming out to see a whole year or multiple years at once.[10]
- Tapping a date reveals the workouts done on that day (or a summary).
- Below or alongside the calendar, a chronological list of workouts with their names, duration, volume, and PRs.[10]
through to a full workout detail screen.
These calendar and streak features are both motivational and useful for adherence tracking.
puts a heavy emphasis on progression analytics, mostly accessed via the Profile → Statistics path:[7][10]
- Top section: last 7 days training summary with a body diagram showing which muscles were trained.[7]
- Tabs or filters for time ranges (last 30 days, 3 months, 1 year, all time; free tier often limited to 3 months).[7]
- Quick link to “Monthly Report” and “Advanced Statistics” sections.[16][10]
### Advanced Statistics: Volume and Muscle Usage
Advanced statistics break down training volume and distribution in several visual ways:[7][10]
- A timeline of recent workouts and a body muscle graphic
- Graph of total sets per muscle group over time (weekly, monthly, yearly resolution).[7]
- Filters for time window: 30 days, 3 months (free), 1 year, all time (Pro).[7]
- Ability to overlay multiple muscle groups on the same chart for comparison.[7]
- Aggregate pie/donut or bar chart of training volume per muscle over a chosen period.[16][7]
- Shows number of workouts, duration, total volume, and sets per muscle group.[7]
according to sets per muscle group.[7]
week navigation to compare distribution over time.[7]
- Ranking of exercises logged the most times, over user
has specific screens focused on a single exercise’s performance over time:[10][7]
- Most reps performed in a set.[7]
- Each KPI has its own trend graph (e.g., 1RM vs time).[7]
- List of records with links to the workouts where they occurred.[7]
- Personal records for specific rep counts (e.g., 5RM, 8RM, 10RM) per exercise.[7]
- For core lifts like barbell squat, bench, and deadlift, shows a “strength level” (Beginner → Elite) comparing the user to others of similar age, weight, and sex.[7]
- Chronological history of all sets or workouts for that exercise.[10][7]
- Tapping any entry opens the full workout session.
These analytics screens are a major part of what users and reviewers call out as “detailed analytics” and “tons of graphs”.[5][6][3]
- Accessed via Profile → Statistics → Monthly Report.[16]
- Shows summary statistics for the previous month: number of workouts, total duration, total volume, total sets.[16]
- Highlights personal records achieved that month.[16]
- List of exercises performed the most.[16]
- Each monthly stat block can be exported as a nicely formatted image to share on social media or saved locally.[16]
and widely praised by users for motivation.[8][5]
Home tab is a social feed of workouts from people you follow:[9][8]
- Workout name and optional description written before logging.[9]
- Duration, training volume, and the number of personal records set.[9]
- Average heart rate if recorded via watch integration.[9]
- Thumbnail or first media (video/photo) associated with the workout, tappable for inline viewing.[9]
- Count of likes and a comment button; tapping opens full comment thread.[8][9]
- Share icon to export a link or visual shareable outside the
- Horizontal carousel of suggested athletes to follow.[8]
- Ability to swipe through attached media without leaving the feed.[9]
Tapping a workout card opens a detailed workout screen:[8]
- Full muscle split, exercises, sets, weights, reps, set types, RPE (if logged), and notes.[8]
- Overall stats: duration, volume, total sets, and PRs achieved.[8]
- Comments, likes, and media gallery, with each media item linked back to its workout.[15][8]
- Option to copy the workout to your own profile or routines list for future use.[6][3][8]
Athlete profile screens expose more detailed social and performance data:[15][8]
- Profile header: avatar, username, bio, follower/following counts, total workouts.[15][8]
week or similar summary of training activity.[8]
- Media grid: list of photos/videos uploaded with workouts; tapping one opens media and links to the associated workout.[15]
- Routines section: routines the athlete has saved or created; you can view details and save to your own library.[8]
- Recent workout feed: list of their latest workouts with tap
allows comparison of your stats with another user:[8]
side metrics: muscle split, number of workouts, total training time, total volume, and movements in common.[8]
range filters (30 days, 3 months, 1 year, all time).[8]
This comparison feature is frequently cited as a motivator for users who enjoy competition.[13][5]
places a lot of focus on generating aesthetic shareable images of your progress:[16]
- Accessed from various stat or workout screens via a “Share” or “Stories” button.[16]
- Fun comparisons (e.g., “You lifted 13,264 kg, like lifting a truck”).[16]
- Options to choose background type (light, dark, transparent).[16]
- Option to export images to gallery or share directly to Instagram Stories and other apps.[16]
- Ability to overlay shareables on gym selfies or lifting clips by using transparent background.[16]
- Users can revisit old workouts in the Profile tab and generate shareables for them as well.[16]
These features reinforce the social loop beyond the in
## Body Metrics, Progress Photos, and Coach Integration
allows users to track gyms progress in multiple dimensions, including body composition:[11][10]
- Inputs for body weight, body fat percentage, girth measurements (e.g., waist, chest, arms, legs).[11]
- Trend graphs for each metric over time.[11]
- Users can upload progress photos and tag them with dates.[11]
- Photos are viewable in a timeline or grid layout and tied to progress tracking.[11]
These same data are accessible to coaches managing clients via
Coach is a companion web/app platform for trainers that integrates with the main
- List of clients with key metrics
user profile but focused on coach
- Trainers can log workouts directly on their own device for the client during 1
1 sessions; these workouts save to the client
assigned programs and workouts, which clients can follow and log.[12]
Coach’s model (separate but connected coach app) is a strong reference.
to export workouts to the Apple Health
- Google Fit / similar integration mentioned by users for syncing training data.[13]
- Automatically pushes gym workouts to Strava, generating a summary that appears on Strava’s feed.[18][13][8]
- Sends a notification for the user to adjust the session before publishing to Strava.[8]
- Apple Watch and Wear OS apps with live sync to phone, heart
rate tracking, rest timers, and complication/tile shortcuts.[3][6]
- Widgets showing last 7 days workout data and calendar.[14]
- Widgets for quick access to “log workout” and day
- Live Activity on lock screen and Dynamic Island integration so users “never lose track” of their workout.[6]
These integrations significantly increase perceived polish and reduce friction for logging.
- **Profile privacy** – choose between public profile and private profile (follow requests required).[6][8]
- **Notifications** – for comments, likes, follow requests, Strava publishing, and messages (in coach context).[12][8]
store documented controls, encrypted data, delete
- **Language localization** – multiple languages supported on iOS, including English and several others.[6]
These are not differentiators alone, but they set expectations for a modern fitness app.
## What Users Love Most (Extracted from Reviews and Discussions)
Aggregating user reviews from Google Play, App Store, Reddit, and third
party blogs reveals repeated themes in what people appreciate about
- Logging sets and reps feels fast, with auto
fill for previous weights and reps removing friction.[6]
- Many users explicitly say it beats spreadsheet tracking because it is more convenient and visually appealing.[21][3]
### Strong Free Tier with Fair Pro Upsell
free, and the free version includes nearly all logging functionality and a good portion of analytics.[19][3]
- Pro mainly unlocks unlimited routines, extended graph history, advanced measurements, and longer time windows in analytics.[6][7]
- Reviewers frequently say Pro is “worth it” and that the free tier already delivers strong value.[19][13]
- “Tons of graphs” and “detailed analytics” are praised as motivating and useful for progressive overload.[20][5][3]
- Users like seeing workout volume, one
rep max progression, total weight lifted, and muscle group distribution.[5][3][7]
- Monthly summaries and comparisons (e.g., “you lifted X tons this week”) create a gamified feel.[3][16]
- The social feed and ability to see friends’ workouts are called out as “super motivating”.[5][3][6]
- Users like copying friends’ workouts and comparing stats.[3][8][6]
- External shareables and Strava integration get positive mentions from users who cross
- Many reviewers like being able to build complex routines, modify them on the fly, and reuse them over weeks.[3][2]
- The exercise library with videos is praised for helping with form; users also like
Coach and the companion client app are highlighted as a “mega incredible” platform for tracking multiple clients, adjusting workouts live, and chatting.[17][12][11]
Negative or constructive feedback appears in user reviews and Reddit threads and can be used to design a differentiated competitor:[21][20][13]
- **Feature creep concerns** – some users explicitly appreciate
simplicity and worry about overcomplication if too many features are added.[21]
- **Pinned notes missing** – multiple users want exercise
level persistent notes that always show, regardless of routine, to remember personal technique cues.[6]
- **Notifications and calendar** – some reviewers request stronger reminder and calendar features (e.g., more flexible habit reminders, workout scheduling).[20]
- **UI rough edges** – a minority mention UI confusion around certain flows or stats; some would like more customization of stats views and widgets.[20]
- **Limited advanced programming** – advanced athletes sometimes want more sophisticated periodization tools or templates beyond the current routine library.[20]
These gaps are opportunities: you can implement pinned notes, richer scheduling, and more advanced programming tools while
## Feature and Screen Checklist for Your Gym Log App
feature set into an actionable checklist organized by priority and module. You can treat it as a product backlog and adapt based on your scope.
- Basic profile (name, avatar, units, gender, training level, primary goals).
- Optional selection of default training split template.
- Start workout button (empty workout or from routine).
- List of exercises for the session.
sets with fields for weight, reps, RPE, and set type (warmup/normal/drop/failure/superset).
- Add/remove sets and swap exercises mid
exercise rest timer with automatic start.
- List of user routines with metadata (split name, days/week, last used).
- Routine editor: add exercises, set order, per
- Ability to duplicate routines and modify copies.
- Core training types supported via flexible configuration (strength, hypertrophy, HIIT, calisthenics).
- Library with filters by muscle group and equipment.
- Exercise detail with video and cues.
- Custom exercise creation with muscle group, equipment, and icon.
- Chronological workout history with stats summary.
- Calendar view showing training days and an active streak indicator.
- Total volume (sum of weight × reps) per workout.
- Number of PRs within a workout.
- Total weight lifted this week/month, with fun equivalent comparisons.
exercise KPIs: heaviest weight, 1RM (calculated), best set volume, best session volume, most reps.
exercise trend graphs for weight, 1RM, and volume over time.
- Set count per muscle group graph with weekly/monthly resolution.
- Muscle distribution chart (pie/bar) for a selected time range.
- Body diagram heatmap for muscles trained over last 7 days / 30 days.
generated monthly summary: workouts, volume, time, sets, top exercises, PRs.
social power, consider the following modules:
- Infinite scroll of workouts from followed users.
- Workout cards with name, description, duration, volume, PR count, average heart rate (if available), and media.
- Share button to export workout or generate a shareable.
- Search for athletes by name or tag.
- Trending or popular workouts and routines.
- Public profile page with avatar, bio, follower/following counts, total workouts.
- Activity graph, media gallery tied to workouts, routines list, recent workouts.
tap copy workout to your account.
- Compare screen showing your stats vs another user (volume, time, muscle split) over selectable time windows.
- Configurable stat cards (PRs, volume, streaks, muscle distribution, monthly report pieces).
- Background modes (light, dark, transparent) and export to stories.
- Weight, body fat, and circumferences with graphs.
- Integration with smart scales / external apps if possible.
- Separate “coach mode” or companion app with:
- Client profiles showing workouts, metrics, and progress photos.
- Ability to assign programs and log workouts for clients.
- Apple Watch / Wear OS app focused on set logging, rest timers, heart rate.
- Optional Strava sync for gym workouts.
widgets: last 7 days stats, calendar, quick
- iOS Live Activity / Android equivalent for active workouts.
- Reminder notifications for planned workout days.
- Localization for key markets (e.g., English + Indian languages for your audience).
Based on gaps in the current
experience, you can deliberately go beyond them:
- **Pinned exercise notes** – persistent notes at the exercise level that appear in every routine where that exercise is used.
- **Richer habit/calendar system** – better scheduling, reminders, and adherence analytics.
configurable dashboards and the ability to pin
local units, vernacular language support, and popular Indian gym splits.
Incorporating these while maintaining the core simplicity of logging and navigation will help your app feel like a next
generation alternative rather than just a clone.
Here’s a detailed feature and screen breakdown based on

that you can use as a blueprint for your own gym log app. The full structured report with citations is attached as a separate document; it covers navigation, analytics, social, and coach features in depth.
At a high level, you should aim to match three pillars that
focuses on: fast workout logging, rich progress tracking (graphs and stats), and a motivating social layer. Below is a concise, implementation
Core App Structure and Main Tabs
Design your navigation around four primary areas (plus settings):
Workout: start workouts, manage routines, browse routine
Profile/Stats: calendar, history, graphs, body metrics, progress
Home/Social: feed of workouts from people the user follows, likes, comments,
Cards for each saved routine (e.g., PPL, upper/lower) with last-used
Free tier can limit number of routines; paid tier unlocks
Mark sets as warmup, normal, drop, failure,
Change exercises and set structure mid
exercise set table: weight, reps, RPE, set
fill previous session weights/reps to reduce typing (users love this).
Automatic rest timer per exercise with
Quick add/remove set and swap exercise
Detail page with video and text
Custom exercises (name, muscles, equipment) that fully integrate into routines and
Filters: level (beginner/intermediate/advanced), goal (muscle gain/strength/fat loss), equipment (gym/dumbbell/home).
Shows overview (level, equipment, duration, days/week) and list of
“Save Program” to add full program to user’s
Categories like At home, Travel, Dumbbells only, Band, Cardio & HIIT, Gym, Bodyweight,
Avatar, bio, total workouts, followers/following (if you add social
Calendar heatmap of training days with an “active streak” (consecutive weeks with at least one workout).
Tapping a day shows workouts done that
List of saved workouts with name, duration, volume, PR count, likes/
These are the analytics screens that make
“Last 7 days” strip with quick stats + a small body diagram showing trained
range filters (30 days, 3 months, 1 year, all time), with some ranges possibly
Set count per muscle group graph (weekly/monthly/yearly) with multiple muscles
Muscle distribution chart (pie/bar) for a period with workouts, duration, volume, sets per
Muscle distribution body view (body heatmap by sets).
logged exercises, tap through to per
KPIs: max weight, projected/true 1RM, best set volume, best session volume, max
Set records (PRs at different rep counts).
level comparison vs population for key lifts (Beginner → Elite).
generate previous month summary: number of workouts, total duration, training volume, sets,
If you want the same engagement:
Cards for workouts from followed users
name, description, duration, volume, PR count, average HR,
Inline media viewer (tap to watch, swipe to see more media) without leaving
Share icon to export link or
Full breakdown of muscle split, exercises, sets, weights, reps, set types, RPE, notes; plus stats and comments/
Profile: avatar, bio, follower/following, total workouts, activity graph, media, routines, recent
Compare screen showing your vs their muscle split, workouts, time trained, volume, common movements for chosen time
PR cards, volume summaries, muscle distribution, monthly report slices, fun equivalent comparisons (“you lifted X, like lifting a truck”).
Background choices (light/dark/transparent) and direct export to stories/other
Weight, body fat %, circumferences; graphs over
Timeline/grid of progress photos tied to
Optional comparison view (e.g., before vs after).
splits this into a companion product (
Build a separate coach app or a “Coach mode” in your app with:
Client profiles mirroring user stats + body metrics +
Ability to log workouts on behalf of client during 1
1 sessions, adjusting sets, rest, and exercises on the
app chat and notifications for messages/program
Wearables: Apple Watch/Wear OS app for set logging, heart
rate display, rest timers, with live sync to
Health platforms: Apple Health / Google Fit export of
Strava: automatic posting of gym workouts to Strava with a summary, editable before
screen widgets showing last 7 days stats, calendar, quick log button, current day
screen / Live Activity: show current workout status and timers on lock screen / Dynamic
Notification preferences (social, reminders, integrations, coach messages
Localization for key languages for your target
Based on user complaints and gaps, these are high
Persistent exercise notes (pinned notes): show personal technique cues every time that exercise appears, across all
Stronger scheduling & reminders: planned workout schedule with smart reminders, streak goals, and adherence stats (beyond just calendar view).
in periodization templates, progression schemes, and auto
Customizable analytics dashboard: let users pin
graphs (e.g., bench 1RM, weekly volume) to a personal stats home screen.
focused features: local units, popular Indian gym splits, Hindi/other language UI, and perhaps integration with local wearables or communities.
All of this is captured in more detail, with citations, in the attached research report titled “
App Deep Feature Analysis for Gym Log App Design.” It lays out the inferred screen flow and feature set module by module so you can convert it directly into your product backlog.
